title: Enchiridion
author: Epictetus
introduction: The Enchiridion ("Handbook") is a compact manual of Stoic ethics distilled from Epictetus's longer Discourses by his student Arrian. In just fifty-three short chapters, it delivers the essence of Stoic practical philosophy: distinguish what you can control from what you cannot, accept the latter with equanimity, and focus all your energy on the former. This pocket-sized guide to inner freedom has been carried by soldiers, statesmen, and seekers for nearly two thousand years.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: What is the key to a tranquil and virtuous life?
argumentCard.thesis: The foundation of all philosophy is this: some things are within our control (our opinions, intentions, desires, and aversions) and some are not (our body, property, reputation, and position) — happiness depends entirely on focusing on the former and accepting the latter.
keyMoves: Opens with the fundamental Stoic distinction: "Some things are in our control and others not" — and all misery comes from trying to control what we cannot
Teaches the discipline of desire: want only what is within your power, and you will never be frustrated; fear only what is within your power, and you will never be afraid
Provides practical exercises: when disturbed by an external event, remind yourself it is not the event but your judgment of it that disturbs you
argumentCard.strongestObjection: The Enchiridion's counsel to remain indifferent to everything external — including the suffering and death of loved ones — can seem not like wisdom but like emotional numbness dressed up as philosophy.

debates.title: What is the path to a good life?
debates.subtitle: Inner virtue vs. the pursuit of pleasure

philosopher1.name: Epictetus
philosopher1.position: Virtue and self-mastery are the only true goods
philosopher1.keyQuote: Seek not that the things which happen should happen as you wish; but wish the things which happen to be as they are, and you will have a tranquil flow of life.
arguments: Everything outside our will — health, wealth, reputation — can be taken away at any moment. Building your happiness on these things is building on sand.
The good life is achieved by aligning desire with what is actually in our power: our judgments, intentions, and responses to the world.
A slave or a prisoner can live the good life; a king who is a slave to his passions cannot. The inner citadel is the only real freedom.

philosopher2.name: Epicurus
philosopher2.position: Pleasure — properly understood — is the highest good
philosopher2.keyQuote: Pleasure is the beginning and the end of the blessed life.
arguments: Nature itself tells us what we value: all living things move toward pleasure and away from pain. To say pleasure is not the good is to fight biology.
But Epicurus is not a hedonist: the highest pleasures are not bodily but intellectual — friendship, philosophical conversation, freedom from fear. These are stable, not fragile.
Denying yourself all pleasure in pursuit of virtue is another kind of bondage — Stoic severity mistakes asceticism for wisdom.

debates.stakes: Both traditions were trying to solve the same problem: how to live well in a world full of uncertainty and loss. Their disagreement about what counts as 'the good' still structures much of modern self-help, psychology, and ethics.
debates.yourTurn: Is Epictetus too detached? Is Epicurus too focused on feeling? Try designing your own answer: what would the good life look like if you combined their insights?
